denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

Aetna has reviewed the prior authorization request for the requested service. After review, the request is denied and the requested service is not approved.

The annual visit limit for outpatient therapy services under this plan has been reached for the current plan year. Additional visits beyond the annual maximum are not a covered benefit this plan year. In addition, the requested service is classified under the habilitative services benefit category, which is excluded from coverage under the terms of this plan.

Coverage of outpatient therapy services is subject to the annual visit maximum and the benefit categories defined in your plan documents, regardless of the provider or setting in which the services are delivered.

This determination applies the plan's standard criteria to all requests of this type and does not reflect any review of the member's diagnosis or individual clinical records. No additional case-specific findings were made.

If you disagree with this determination, you may file an internal appeal by contacting the number on your member identification card within the timeframe stated in your plan documents. You may submit additional information with that request.

This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Aetna Utilization Management
clinical_context: The patient is a 34-year-old man who underwent arthroscopic partial medial meniscectomy of the right knee eight weeks ago and was referred for a continued course of post-operative physical therapy to restore quadriceps strength, range of motion, and gait mechanics that were lost after the injury and surgery. Because this therapy restores function the patient previously had, it is rehabilitative care, not habilitative care, which develops new functional skills a patient never acquired; the denial's classification of the therapy under the habilitative services benefit category is therefore incorrect. His orthopedic surgeon documented persistent quadriceps weakness, a 15-degree extension lag, and an antalgic gait, and recommended additional supervised physical therapy beyond the plan's annual visit cap to complete the recovery. The denial letter contains no reference to his diagnosis, his surgeon, the operative report, or any of the submitted progress notes, and states that it does not reflect any review of the individual records. The operative report and the physical therapy progress notes were submitted with the prior authorization request.

scenario_realism.reasoning: Aetna denying continued post-op PT on an annual visit cap is a common commercial dispute; boilerplate no-individual-review letter and rehabilitative-miscoded-as-habilitative are realistic exploitable defects.

insurer_voice.reasoning: Correct Aetna branding and cool procedural cadence, no wrong-insurer tells, but no named Aetna CPB/InterQual fingerprint (intentionally generic for the boilerplate flaw).

scope_guard.reasoning: Aetna in-scope, fully-insured commercial, adult 34, member-facing internal appeal only, no live-filing language.

appeal_difficulty.reasoning: Boilerplate denial admitting no individual review despite submitted records, plus rehabilitative PT miscoded as excluded habilitative; easy multi-vector appeal.
exploitable_weaknesses: Therapy misclassified as habilitative when it is rehabilitative (restoring previously-held function after meniscectomy), making the exclusion inapplicable
